coredata.dc:title: Concentration effect on optical properties of carbon dots at room temperature
coredata.dc:creator: Isnaeni,
Herbani, Yuliati
Suliyanti, Maria Margaretha
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are interesting fluorescent nanoparticles to study due to their excelent optical properties and potential applications. However, further exciton study must be done to reveal luminescent origin. In this work, we present exciton dynamic study of carbon dots at room temperature. The carbon dots sample was synthesized from commercial sugar using simple microwave-assisted technique. Photoluminescence study was carefully done using various techniques, such as down and up conversion photoluminescence, time-resolved photoluminescence. Additional measurement such as absorbance spectroscopy and transmission electron microscope (TEM) were utilized. From TEM image, we found that carbon dots have lattice constant approximately 2.94 Angstrom. Furthermore, we can distinguish energy transition in carbon dots, such as core and surface energy, using absorbance and photoluminescence spectra. We found that surface energy become dominant as carbon dots concentration increase. Higher carbon dots concentration shift surface energy of carbon dots. However, this shift was not observed in up-conversion photoluminescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Surface energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Kegiatan Unggulan and Kegiatan Penguatan Kompetensi Inti 2016-2017 from Indonesian Institute of Sciences and partly supported by Kegiatan Insinas 2017 from Kementrian Ristekdikti Republic of Indonesia .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental setup of photluminescence (a) and up-conversion photoluminescence (b).
TEM image of fabricated carbon dot using microwave technique.
Absorbance spectra of carbon dots at various concentration (a), analysis of absorbance spectra using multi-peak fitting (b) and absorbance peak shift of various concentration of carbon dots (c).

Photoluminescence spectra of carbon dots at various concentrations (a), photoluminescence intensity and peak wavelength as function of carbon dots concentration (b), multi-peak fitting of carbon dots photoluminescence spectrum (c) and shift of E1 and E2 (d).
Time-resolved photoluminescence of carbon dots at various concentrations (a) and decay life-time and rise-time as function of carbon dots concentrations (b).
Up-conversion photoluminescence of carbon dots at various concentrations (a) and multi-peak fitting of two photon spectrum (b).

head.ce:title: Concentration effect on optical properties of carbon dots at room temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Physics, Indonesian Institute of Sciences, Building 442 Puspiptek, South Tangerang, Banten 15314, Indonesia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Physics, Indonesian Institute of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are interesting fluorescent nanoparticles to study due to their excelent optical properties and potential applications. However, further exciton study must be done to reveal luminescent origin. In this work, we present exciton dynamic study of carbon dots at room temperature. The carbon dots sample was synthesized from commercial sugar using simple microwave-assisted technique. Photoluminescence study was carefully done using various techniques, such as down and up conversion photoluminescence, time-resolved photoluminescence. Additional measurement such as absorbance spectroscopy and transmission electron microscope (TEM) were utilized. From TEM image, we found that carbon dots have lattice constant approximately 2.94 Angstrom. Furthermore, we can distinguish energy transition in carbon dots, such as core and surface energy, using absorbance and photoluminescence spectra. We found that surface energy become dominant as carbon dots concentration increase. Higher carbon dots concentration shift surface energy of carbon dots. However, this shift was not observed in up-conversion photoluminescence.

ce:section.ce:para: Research on fluorescent nanoparticles has been rapidly grown due to emerging new types of fluorescent nanoparticles, such as semiconductor quantum dots, graphene dots, carbon dots, etc. Carbon dots, which were discovered last decade, have shown good performances, so that they are potential for many applications [1,2]. Although carbon dots consist of carbon atoms, but their optical and electrical properties are unlike their counterparts, such as graphene dots, carbon nanotubes and polymer dots. Carbon dots are described as carbon atoms that form spherical nanostructures below 10 nm [2]. Although their electrical property is not their strength, they are well known as good fluorescent nanoparticles; [2,3]. Carbon dots become very popular as fluorescent nanoparticles due to not only their good optical properties, but also simple synthesis procedure. Carbon dots are easily made of various natural sources using several well known techniques, such as hydrothermal, solvothermal, ultrasonicating and microwave-assisted techniques [3,4].

These easy synthesis procedures have stimulated many researchers to study carbon dots. Studies related to optical properties of carbon dots become very important to understand and reveal the origin of luminescence and further applications of carbon dots. Several researchers have proved that carbon dots are applicable several applications, such as solar cell, light emitting diodes, bio-tagging, biomedical, anti-counterfeiting labels and many more [3]. Many studies on optical properties of carbon dots, such as (down-conversion) photoluminescence, up-conversion photoluminescence, time-resolved photoluminescence, phosphorescence and multi-color luminescent, have been done to reveal the origin of luminescence of carbon dots. Until now, there are two main opinions related to the origin of carbon luminescence. First opinion suggests that size of carbon dots significantly influences their luminescent [5,6]. This option is based on several previous studies on graphene dots and semiconductor quantum dots. Second option proposes that luminescent of carbon dots is due to their surface energy level [7,8]. This opinion comes from the fact that type of solvents and functional groups strongly affect luminescent of carbon dots.

Regardless those opinions, there is an interesting fact has been discovered that luminescent of carbon dots can be tuned by changing excitation wavelength [3,7]. Changing the excitation wavelength from shorter wavelength to longer wavelength causes the luminescent of carbon dots change from blue color to red color. This multi color luminescent of carbon dot opens possible applications in light emitting diodes and photovoltaic devices. In addition to multi excitation wavelength, one study has reveals that different thickness of carbon dots layer produce different luminescent color [9]. However, there was no further explanation regarding this issue. The change of luminescent spectrum due to concentration has been observed in organic dyes, such as Rhodamine [10]. The concentration of Rhodamine dye significantly changes luminescent spectrum due to change of dye structure from monomer to dimer. Thus, it is still a question whether carbon dots have similar optical properties as organic dyes. Therefore, a study on concentration dependence of carbon dots luminescent must be done to reveal exciton dynamics in carbon dots. The purpose of this work is to observe luminescent spectra change of carbon dots in different concentration to expose and confirm the main origin of carbon dots luminescent at room temperature. In particular, we would like to observe optical properties of carbon dots made of sugar using microwave-assisted technique.

Carbon dots were made of sugar and synthesized using simple microwave-assisted technique. 15 g of commercial white sugar, which was purchased from local market, was dissolved in 100 ml water using magnetic stirrer. The sugar solution was then placed in microwave oven at power 450 Watt for 40 min to yield dark brown dried carbon dots. After that, 100 ml water was used to dissolve dried carbon dots. The solution was centrifuged to separate supernatant and precipitate. The supernatant became carbon dots colloid solution sample. The concentration of carbon dot in the solvent was measured using micro balance. We obtain the concentration of original carbon dots was 12.8 mg/ml. We produce 10 different concentration of carbon dots solution by diluting the synthesized carbon dot solution.

Photoluminescence and time-resolved photoluminescence were conducted simultaneously as illustrated in Fig. 1 . A pico-second laser at 420 nm was focused and used to excite carbon dots sample. Emission from carbon dots was collected by two plano-convex lenses. A beam splitter was use to divide emission beam into a fiber optic input for photoluminescence measurement and into a photon counting detector module for time-resolved photoluminescence measurement (Fig. 1a). To study up-conversion photoluminescence, femto-second laser at wavelength 780 nm, repetition rate 40 MHz and power 1.5 Watt was directed and focused on carbon dot samples using a 10X objective lens. The emission was collected sideway using two plano-convex lenses and detected by fiber optic based spectrometer to obtain emission spectrum (Fig. 1b). All optical measurements were conducted in ambient atmosphere and room temperature. Additional measurement used in this work were UV–Vis–NIR absorbance spectroscopy and transmission electron microscope (TEM).

Carbon dots were successfully synthesized using simple microwave-assisted technique. TEM image (as shown in Fig. 2 ) reveals clear structure of synthesized carbon dots. The shape of carbon dots are almost solid with size ranging from 3 nm until 7 nm. We have calculated that the atomic lattice is approximately 2.94 Angstrom, which is typical atomic lattice of carbon atoms. We found that some carbon dots have irregular shape due to uncontrollable synthesis process. We also found that using microwave-assisted technique, it is difficult to have monodispersed nanoparticles. Aggregation of carbon dots in this process is inevitable since the sugar liquid was completely dried in the microwave oven. Further technique is required to avoid aggregation of carbon dots. Nevertheless, using simple microwave-assisted technique, we can synthesize water soluble carbon dots that remain perfectly dissolved in water several months after synthesis. Water solubility of carbon dots is very important for further application of carbon dots, such as for bio-imaging and photonic devices.

Absorbance spectra of carbon dots at various concentrations are given in Fig. 3 a. We found that carbon dots concentration significantly changes absorbance spectrum. At low concentration (0.025 mg/ml) we found three distinctive absorbance peaks at wavelength 229 nm, 275 nm and 324 nm. However, at high concentration (12.8 mg/ml), we found dominant absorbance peak at 573 nm. In order to clarify this change, we analyzed all absorbance spectra using Gaussian fitting. Every spectrum was perfectly fitted by three Gaussian curves as shown in Fig. 3.b. Gaussian fitting was selected since it accurately described energy distribution in organic materials. Based on fitting results, we found that the first peak (noted as P1 in Fig. 3b and c) remains stable for all carbon dots concentration. Absorbance peak only slightly shifts from 229 nm (for low carbon dot concentration) to 240 nm (for high carbon dot concentration). The similar trend was found for second absorbance peak (P2). This absorbance peak shifts from 275 nm (for low carbon dot concentration) to 302 nm (for high carbon dot concentration). The last absorbance peak (P3) significantly shifts from 324 nm (for low carbon dots concentration) to 573 nm (for high carbon dots concentration).

We assume that the first peak (P1) is related to
σ −
σ
*
energy transition, and the second peak is originated from
π −
π
*
energy transition in carbon dots [11–13]. Both
σ −
σ
*
and
π −
π
*
energy transitions come from the core of carbon dots. Therefore, these absorbance peaks are not significantly influenced by concentration. The last peak is related to n -
π
*
energy transition, which is originated from the surface of carbon dots [11–13]. This absorbance peak is strongly affected by concentration. In this case, different concentration of carbon dots related to number of carbon dots in the samples and relative distance between carbon dot nanoparticles, regardless aggregation effect that occurs in this sample. At high carbon dots concentration (12.8 mg/ml) the relative distance between carbon dot nanoparticles is very small, so that the exciton annihilation may take place in carbon dots [11–13]. Surface energy bands from individual carbon dots may overlap one to another creating new energy level. These new energy bands have lower energy than that of individual carbon dots, so that the absorbance peak experiences red shift, which is shifting to longer wavelength. Exciton annihilation also may occur in energy bands originated from the core of carbon dots (
σ −
σ
*
and
π −
π
*
energy transitions) due to small relative distance between carbons dots. However, the energy shift is not significant as shown in absorbance spectrum.

We found that
π −
π
*
energy transition is easily observed in low concentration samples, but when carbon dots concentration become higher,
π −
π
*
energy transition become difficult to observe. At low carbon dot concentration,
π −
π
*
energy transition is dominant. However,after carbon dot concentration reaches 0.8 mg/ml, n -
π
*
energy transition became dominant. At low concentration
σ −
σ
*
energy transition is dominant than n -
π
*
energy transition. At high concentration, surface-surface interaction between carbon dots become stronger and dominant. The change of n -
π
*
energy transition was previously found in laser ablated carbon dots [14].

The red shift of absorbance spectrum is considered to have influence in photoluminescence spectra (as shown in Fig. 4 ). In this work, various concentration of carbon dots samples were excited using continuous wave laser at 420 nm wavelength as indicated in Fig. 4a. As carbon dots concentration increases, photoluminescence intensity increases and peak wavelength shifts to longer wavelength as shown in Fig. 4b. Peak wavelength shifts from 518 nm for low carbon dots concentration to 547 nm for high carbon dots concentration. The shift of peak wavelength is approximately linear to carbon dot concentration, but photoluminescence intensity is approximately linear to carbon dots concentration only up to 1.6 mg/ml. The photoluminescence intensity tends to saturate at high carbon dots concentration. This saturation is probably due to re-absorption process that happen between carbon dots in high concentration.

The shift of emission peak wavelength is correlated to absorbance peak shift due to concentration. Since the photoluminescence spectra of carbon dots are not Gaussian, thus we conducted multi-Gaussian fitting to all photoluminescence spectra. We assumed that a photoluminescence spectrum consists of at least two Gaussian spectra, which give detail information of luminescent origin. We found that each photoluminescence spectrum can always be fitted by two Gaussian curves as shown in Fig. 4c. The first Gaussian spectrum (E1) has shorter peak wavelength than that that second Gaussian spectrum (E2) as shown in Fig. 4d. We fitted photoluminescence spectra for all carbon dots concentrations. We found that both E1 and E2 experience red shift (move to longer wavelength) as carbon dot concentration increases as shown in Fig. 4d. E1 spectrum shifts from 517 nm for low carbon dots concentration to 536 nm for high carbon dot concentration. Likewise, E2 spectrum significantly shifts from 538 nm for low carbon dots concentration to 602 nm for high carbon dot concentration. Peak wavelength shift of E2 is more noticeable than that of E1. We assume that photoluminescence of E1 is originated from radiative recombination of core of carbon dots, which is from
π −
π
*
energy transition. The change of peak wavelength shift is slightly observed and comparable to shift of absorbance peak mentioned earlier. On the other hand, E2 spectrum is originated from surface level of carbon dots, since the shift of photoluminescence peak is comparable to absorbance peak shift. We also notice that there is Stoke shift occur in the process of radiative recombination of carbon dots surface level.

Time-resolved photoluminescence of carbon dots samples at various concentrations was done using pico-second laser 420 nm wavelength as excitation source. Since we used 500 nm long-pass filter, thus lifetime curves was measured for emission wavelength above 500 nm as shown in Fig. 5 a. The inset of Fig. 5a clearly shows IRF (instrument response function) and lifetime curves of all samples near IRF signal. Every lifetime signal was then fitting using two-exponential decay, and the average lifetime (average) was calculated using following equation [15].
(1)
τ
average
=
Σ (
α
i
τ
i
2
)
Σ (
α
i
τ
i
)
where α and τ are intensity and lifetime coefficient of each exponential decay fitting.

Rise time (as shown in Fig. 5b) indicates time required for electron to reach relaxation level before recombination process. From Fig. 5b, it is clearly seen that rise time remain stable until carbon dot concentration 0.8 mg/ml, and then suddenly drops at higher carbon concentration. This result is strongly correlated to absorbance peak of surface level (P3 in Fig. 3c). When carbon concentration higher than 0.8 mg/ml, surface level become dominant, thus the excited electrons tend to find relaxation energy level in this dominated energy level before starting recombination process. Surface energy level of carbon dots at high carbon dots concentration become lower, wherein exciton tends to reach this energy level easily probably due to exciton annihilation. Therefore, there is a clear correlation between rise time and surface level. This result indicates that for carbon dots at high concentration, emission of carbon dot is originated from surface energy level.

The average lifetime decreases as carbon dots concentration increases as shown in Fig. 5b. This result clearly indicates that recombination process and emission origin is strongly correlated to surface energy level. This results also suggests that as carbon dots concentration increases, non-radiative recombination dominates over radiative recombination due to exciton annihilation [14].

Furthermore, we conducted up-conversion photoluminescence using fs-laser at wavelength 780 nm as excitation source. We found that for all carbon dot concentrations, there is no emission peak shift that can be observed. We only found that emission intensity increases as carbon dots concentration increases. We observed that for each carbon dots concentration, its emission spectrum can be fitted using two Gaussian fitting. The first peak is located near 560 nm wavelength (= 2.21 eV) and second peak is located at 600 nm wavelength (= 2.06 eV). We assume that the first peak indicates smallest band gap in the core of carbon dot (
π −
π
*
transition) and the second peak indicates smallest band gap in the surface energy level (n -
π
*
transition) (Fig. 6 ).

Carbon dots made of sugar were successfully synthesized using microwave-assisted technique. The size of carbon dots are approximately 7 – 9 nm. From absorbance spectroscopy, photoluminescence and time-resolved photoluminescence, we found that concentration of carbon dots significantly affects emission spectrum and optical properties. We are able to distinguish energy transition from core and surface. Emission from core energy transition is slightly influenced by carbon dot concentration. On the other hand, surface energy transition is strongly influenced by concentration of carbon dots. Higher concentration of carbon dots induces emission from surface energy transition. This result is very useful to study exciton dynamics in carbon dots. We conclude that surface energy transition is the main origin of carbon dots luminescent. However, we could not found the effect of concentration on up-conversion photoluminescence. Further study is required to clarify the exciton dynamics in carbon dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Kegiatan Unggulan and Kegiatan Penguatan Kompetensi Inti 2016-2017 from Indonesian Institute of Sciences and partly supported by Kegiatan Insinas 2017 from Kementrian Ristekdikti Republic of Indonesia.
